title: Two univ students killed as DNCC truck rams motorcycle
text:
Two univ students killed as DNCC truck rams motorcycle
Two university students were killed after their motorcycle was rammed by a Dhaka North City Corporation (DNCC) garbage truck in the Konapara area under Demra Police Station in Dhaka.
The deceased were identified as 25-year-olds Iram Redwan and Apu Ahmed. Iram was a student at the American International University-Bangladesh (AIUB), while Apu studied at United International University (UIU).
Following the accident on Thursday night, both were rescued in critical condition and taken to the Emergency Department of Dhaka Medical College Hospital (DMCH), where doctors declared them dead early Friday.
Tausif, a friend who brought the injured students to the hospital, said Iram and Apu were returning home on a motorcycle after attending a wedding event. When they reached the Konapara area of Demra, a DNCC garbage truck rammed their motorcycle. After receiving information, locals rescued them in severely injured condition and took them to DMCH. Apu died first, followed shortly afterward by Iram.
Both students lived in the Chittagong Road area under Demra Police Station. The truck involved in the incident (Dhaka Metro-Sha-14-0574) has been seized.
Md Faruk, in-charge (inspector) of the DMCH Police Camp, confirmed the deaths and said the bodies have been kept in the DMCH morgue. The concerned police station has been informed.

title: Shibir's GS candidate pledges safe campus for female students
text:
Shibir's GS candidate pledges safe campus for female students
Sayed Bin Habib, the General Secretary candidate of the Combined Student Alliance backed by Chhatra Shibir in the Chittagong University Central Students' Union (CUCSU) as well as hall and hostel union elections, has expressed his commitment to creating a safe campus for female students if elected.
Speaking to the media on Wednesday (October 15) around 10:00 am at the IT Building while casting his vote, he said, "Voting is a strictly personal right of the students. They will elect whoever they consider worthy, and this decision is entirely theirs."
He added, "If the students elect me, I will work in a student-friendly manner, Inshallah. We will continue to work for the welfare of all students. I will strive to build a beautiful campus full of dreams and transform the university into a truly academic environment."

title: Direct flights between Agartala-Bagdogra and Agartala-Silchar to boost air connectivity
text:
Agartala, Oct 26 : Direct flight services connecting Agartala with Bagdogra in West Bengal will begin on Sunday evening, while air connectivity between Agartala and Silchar in southern Assam will resume on October 28 after nearly three decades, officials said.
Assistant General Manager of the Airports Authority of India (AAI), Agartala, S. Haokip Jempu, said that Air India Express will commence its AgartalaГўвӮ¬"Bagdogra service on Sunday evening from the Maharaja Bir Bikram (MBB) Airport in Agartala.
Bagdogra Airport, located in Siliguri in North Bengal, is about 68 km from the popular tourist destination Darjeeling. He said that flight services between Agartala and Silchar will begin from October 28, operating twice a week, every Tuesday and Friday.
The route will be operated by IndiGo, marking the resumption of air services between the two Northeastern cities after almost 30 years.
With these additions, Agartala will now have direct air connectivity with 10 destinations across the country. At present, the Tripura capital is connected by air to Kolkata, Delhi, Imphal, Bengaluru, Hyderabad, Dibrugarh, Guwahati, and Chennai.
According to AAI officials, Agartala's MBB Airport ranks 10th in the country in terms of the customer satisfaction index among all airports in India.
Currently, 13 to 16 flights operate daily, handling around 4,000 to 5,000 passengers per day.
The newly-constructed terminal building at the MBB Airport, with a built-up area of 30,000 sq. metres, has been designed to handle both domestic and international passengers simultaneously during peak hours.
Agartala's MBB Airport is the second busiest airport in the Northeast, after the Lokpriya Gopinath Bordoloi International (LGBI) Airport in Guwahati. There are a total of 23 airports in the northeastern region, of which 17 are operational. These include Guwahati, Silchar, Dibrugarh, Jorhat, Tezpur, Lilabari and Rupsi (Assam); Tezu, Pasighat, Ziro and Donyi Polo Airport (Arunachal Pradesh); Agartala (Tripura); Imphal (Manipur); Shillong (Meghalaya); Dimapur (Nagaland); Lengpui (Mizoram); and Pakyong (Sikkim).
Meanwhile, Tripura Transport and Tourism Minister Sushanta Chowdhury earlier said that the state government had decided to extend financial support for the proposed AgartalaГўвӮ¬"Chittagong (Bangladesh) air service to be operated by SpiceJet.
He said the government had allocated Rs 15 crore to the AAI for the project and already paid Rs 3.85 crore as a three-month advance in December 2022, besides deploying 25 police personnel to assist the Bureau of Immigration under the Ministry of Home Affairs.
However, due to political unrest in Bangladesh since August last year, the launch of flight services between Agartala and Chittagong has been delayed.
According to historian and writer Pannalal Roy, the land for Agartala, Kailashahar, and several other (now defunct) airports in Tripura was donated by the stateГўвӮ¬TMs erstwhile royal family.
"During the Second World War, King Bir Bikram supported the Allied powers, especially Great Britain, and deployed a contingent of the Tripura Army to assist them. During that time, airports at Agartala, Kailashahar, and other places were built to facilitate the Allied forces," Roy, who has authored several books on Tripura's royal history, told IANS.
He added that in 1943, Japanese fighter planes twice bombarded the Agartala airport.

title: In counter move, Bangladesh puts visa operations on hold in India
text:
In counter move, Bangladesh puts visa operations on hold in India
The tit-for-tat move comes in the wake of unrest across Bangladesh following the death of Sharif Osman Hadi, a leader of the protests that led to the ouster of Sheikh Hasina's Awami League government in 2024.
PTI reported that the Bangladesh Assistant High Commission in Tripura had also suspended visa services.
Citing "unavoidable circumstances", a notice issued by the Bangladesh High Commission here said: "All consular and visa services at the Bangladesh High Commission in New Delhi have been temporarily suspended until further notice. Any inconvenience caused is sincerely regretted."
The tit-for-tat move comes in the wake of unrest across Bangladesh following the death of Sharif Osman Hadi, a leader of the protests that led to the ouster of Sheikh Hasina's Awami League government in 2024.
In its statement on Sunday, the Indian Visa Application Centre, Bangladesh, had cited a "security incident" for suspending services. "Due to a recent security incident at AHCI (Assistant High Commission of India) Chittagong, Indian visa operations at IVAC Chittagong will remain suspended from 21/12/2025 until further notice," it said. "The announcement for reopening the visa centre will be made after reviewing the situation," it said.
Meanwhile, India and Bangladesh have also countered each other on a reported protest outside the Bangladesh High Commission in New Delhi. While India has denied any security breach, Bangladesh has "entirely rejected" the statement.
"We have noted misleading propaganda in sections of the Bangladesh media on the incident. The fact is that about 20-25 youth gathered in front of the Bangladesh High Commission in New Delhi on December 20 and raised slogans in protest against the horrendous killing of Dipu Chandra Das in Mymensingh, while also calling for the protection of all minorities in Bangladesh," MEA spokesperson Randhir Jaiswal said on Sunday.
"There was no attempt to breach the fence or create a security situation at any time. The police stationed at the spot dispersed the group after a few minutes. Visual evidence of these events is available publicly for all to see. India is committed to ensure the safety of foreign missions/ posts in its territory in accordance with the Vienna Convention," he said.
"India continues to keep a close watch on the evolving situation in Bangladesh. Our officials remain in touch with Bangladesh authorities and have conveyed to them our strong concerns at the attacks on minorities. We have also urged that the perpetrators of the barbaric killing of Das be brought to justice," Jaiswal said.
Das, a 27-year-old Hindu youth, was beaten to death by a mob over alleged blasphemy, and his body was subsequently set on fire on December 18.
Rejecting India's statement, Bangladesh questioned how the protesters were allowed to come so close in a secured diplomatic enclave. "Regarding the Indian press note, we completely reject it, entirely reject it. The issue has been presented as if it were very simple, whereas in reality it is not," state-run news agency Bangladesh Sangbad Sangstha (BSS) had quoted the interim government's foreign affairs adviser M Touhid Hossain as saying at a media briefing.
Story continues below this ad
He questioned how a group of 25 to 30 people, "described as belonging to a Hindu extremist organisation, could reach such a sensitive zone," adding that under normal circumstances this should not have been possible "unless they were allowed the access."
Divya A reports on travel, tourism, culture and social issues - not necessarily in that order - for The Indian Express. She's been a journalist for over a decade now, working with Khaleej Times and The Times of India, before settling down at Express. Besides writing/ editing news reports, she indulges her pen to write short stories. As Sanskriti Prabha Dutt Fellow for Excellence in Journalism, she is researching on the lives of the children of sex workers in India. ... Read More

title: Bangladesh Suspends Visa Operations in Delhi
text:
Bangladesh Suspends Visa Operations in Delhi
Government sources in New Delhi said visa services currently stand suspended only at the Indian mission in Chittagong, while they are operational at other Indian missions in Bangladesh, including at the capital, Dhaka.
Bangladesh has suspended visa operations at its High Commission in New Delhi and at its mission in Agartala, the capital of Tripura, diplomatic sources confirmed on Tuesday evening.
Meanwhile, government sources in New Delhi clarified that visa services are currently suspended only at the Indian mission in Chittagong, while they remain operational at other Indian missions in Bangladesh, including in the capital, Dhaka. Visa services at the Chittagong mission were earlier suspended following violent protests by radical groups, during which mobs allegedly hurled stones and other projectiles at the Indian mission.
According to reports, thousands of Indian medical students are enrolled in institutions in Bangladesh. However, many of them have returned to India temporarily amid growing concerns over the deteriorating law-and-order situation in the neighbouring country.

title: 4 injured in clash outside Indian mission in Ctg
text:
4 injured in clash outside Indian mission in Ctg
At least four people, including two police personnel, have been injured in a clash between the protesters and the police outside the Indian Assistant High Commission in Chattogram following the death of Sharif Osman bin Hadi, spokesperson of Inqilab Mancha.
The protesters gathered outside the mission's office in the city's Khulshi area on Thursday night, December 18, and hurled bricks and vandalised the office premises around 1:00 am, said police.
When police intervened, a chase and counter-chase ensued between the law enforcers and the protesters, said Chattogram Metropolitan Police (CMP) Commissioner Hasib Aziz.
The injured were admitted to Chittagong Medical College Hospital, he said.
Police detained 12 people from the spot, and they would be shown arrested under the Anti-Terrorism Act, the CMP Commissioner added.
Meanwhile, a section of the protesters remained in front of Khulshi police station until late at night, demanding the release of the detainees.
Earlier, students and activists under the banner of Inqilab Mancha staged a protest rally at Soloshahar No. 2 Gate, demanding punishment for those involved in Hadi's killing.
Officer-in-charge of Panchlaish police station, Md Solaiman, said around 200 protesters gathered in Soloshahar and No. 2 Gate area to protest the killing of Osman Hadi.
They later marched toward Mayor Goli and set a motorcycle on fire at the house of former education minister and Awami League leader Mohibul Hasan Chowdhury Naufel, he said.
Source: UNB

title: Testing times for India-Bangladesh ties amid deepening rifts
text:
Testing times for India-Bangladesh ties amid deepening rifts
December 25, 2025Disputes over minority safety and diplomatic security have triggered retaliatory visa freezes between India and Bangladesh, disrupting medical travel and signaling a sharp deterioration in relations.
Diplomatic relations between India and Bangladesh have hit a critical juncture with Dhaka suspending visa and consular services at its missions in New Delhi and Agartala on December 22 following protests outside the facilities.
India has also suspended visa services at its Chittagong visa application center in Bangladesh following widespread violent protests triggered by the death of prominent Bangledeshi activist Sharif Osman Hadi.
The 32-year-old was a fierce critic of India and a key figure in the 2024 uprising that ended the 15-year autocratic rule of former Bangladeshi Prime Minister Sheikh Hasina.
Hadi died on December 18 in a Singapore hospital where he was being treated after being shot in the head a week earlier in Dhaka by a masked man riding on the back of a motorbike.
Bangladesh's interim leader Muhammad Yunus has called the shooting a premeditated attack carried out by a powerful network aimed at derailing elections planned for February.
Envoys summoned in tit-for-tat move
Tensions between India and Bangladesh further increased following the killing of Dipu Chandra Das, a 25-year-old Hindu man lynched and burned in public in Bangladesh's Mymensingh district over blasphemy allegations.
Several areas across India, including Kolkata and Hyderabad, witnessed protests by prominent Hindu groups seeking justice for Das. Many carried placards and banners with slogans including "India will not tolerate torture of Hindus in Bangladesh."
New Delhi and Dhaka summoned each other's envoys earlier this week in a tit-for-tat move to express concern over the situation that has escalated into reciprocal suspension of visa services and heightened tensions over minority safety and diplomatic security.
This visa freeze affects tens of thousands of Bangladeshis seeking medical treatment in India, creating a humanitarian dimension.
India has sharply rejected Bangladesh's attempt to equate protests at its facilities in Dhaka with protests outside Bangladeshi missions in India.
"India continues to keep a close watch on the evolving situation in Bangladesh. Our officials remain in touch with Bangladesh authorities and have conveyed to them our strong concerns at the attacks on minorities. We have also urged that the perpetrators of the barbaric killing of Das be brought to justice," said Randhir Jaiswal, spokesperson for India's Foreign Ministry.
The visa suspension represents the most tangible impact on ordinary citizens since tensions flared. India was issuing around 1,500 visas daily to Bangladeshis, prioritizing medical and emergency visas.
The entire pipeline of patients is affected especially for those needing new visas, follow-up appointments and emergency care.
But this is not the first time this kind of thing has happened. Following political unrest and Hasina's ouster in August last year, Indian visa centers across the country were closed indefinitely for security reasons.
'Complex interdependence'
Experts and former diplomats emphasize that the diplomatic breakdown between the neighboring nations was serious given the recent tensions.
"The visa freeze is ad hoc, not permanent," Sanjay Bhardwaj, a professor of South Asian studies at Delhi's Jawaharlal Nehru University, told DW, noting that it is "more of a tactical move rather than a strategic rupture."
"India closed its Chittagong visa office after escalating anti-India rhetoric threatened staff safety. Bangladesh's suspension of visa operations in India is purely retaliatory, designed to appease domestic constituencies," he said.
Bhardwaj said that India and Bangladesh share a complex interdependence.
"Bangladesh cannot function without Indian cooperation - whether in trade, transit, or connectivity," he added.
"This visa standoff, while disruptive for ordinary citizens, does not automatically derail broader bilateral relations or commerce. Both sides understand the stakes. The freeze hurts people, not the relationship's structural foundation."
Medical travel hit by fallout
This latest flashpoint builds on over a year of fraying ties that have deteriorated sharply since Sheikh Hasina's ouster, with India expressing growing alarm over the safety of Hindus and other minorities in Bangladesh while Dhaka has pushed back against what it perceives as interference in its internal affairs.
"India's visa stoppage was a temporary security measure responding to tangible, credible threats against our missions and visa centers. Services have since resumed everywhere except Chittagong where the threat remains real," Pinak Ranjan Chakravarty, a former high commissioner to Bangladesh, told DW.
Chakravarty also believes Bangladesh's response is just retaliation. "They're manufacturing a false equivalence to claim their missions in India face similar dangers. It is a fabricated narrative for domestic consumption, not grounded in comparable security concerns," he said.
"Yes, summoning high commissioners is standard diplomatic protocol for lodging concerns. But there is a difference between legitimate security threats and political posturing," added Chakravarty.
Sreeradha Datta, a Bangladesh expert at the Jindal School of International Affairs, said the current crisis will de-escalate in days, but normal India-Bangladesh relations will not resume until an elected government takes power in Dhaka.
"Fringe elements have hijacked the political narrative - this does not reflect majority Bangladeshi sentiment," Datta told DW.
"The interim government must prove it can control internal violence. What happened with Prothom Alo and Daily Star newspapers exposes either gross incompetence or ill intention, and the authorities have shown little interest in repairing ties with India," added Datta.
On December 18, mobs vandalized, looted, and set fire to the headquarters of the newspaper offices amid riots following the assassination of youth leader Hadi.
"The stalemate is clear. Bangladesh's caretaker administration lacks legitimacy and political will, while India waits for a credible partner. Unless Dhaka signals genuine intent to normalize relations, New Delhi will not budge," said Datta.
What next for India-Bangladesh ties?
Bangladesh's interim government blamed the violence on fringe elements and vowed full justice for the attacks.
A culture of mutual blame at both public and diplomatic levels has deteriorated ties between India and Bangladesh.
Some experts say the relationship is clearly in crisis mode with visa services suspended, envoys being summoned, protests at diplomatic missions, and a breakdown in day-to-day cooperation.
Riva Ganguly Das, a former envoy to Bangladesh, told DW that the latest round of protests leading to visa suspensions has escalated India-Bangladesh tensions to dangerous levels.
She said that given the situation in Bangladesh and the threat to Indian visa offices, closing the office seems the only alternative.
"The security situation in Bangladesh is extremely serious, bordering on lawlessness. For Dhaka to compare protests in India with the violence in Bangladesh is fundamentally disingenuous," said Das.
"Also, the contradictions are glaring," she added.
"Following the killing of youth leader Hadi ... police claim his assassins may still be inside the country, while other government officials simultaneously allege they have fled to India," she added.
Despite the current acrimony, experts suggest political stability offers the clearest path out of this crisis.
The stage, said Datta, was set for Bangladesh Nationalist Party (BNP) leader Tarique Rahman's return to the country after 17 years in exile.
"There is a deliberate attempt by certain sections to vitiate and polarize the situation ahead of the February elections. However, Rahman's return could bring a stronger push for stability and keeping elections on schedule," said Datta.
Edited by: Keith Walker
